todays_post.title: Sunday March 16 | TAM | PAS | Strange | CTA: C
todays_post.content:
# Sunday March 16 | TAM | PAS | Strange | CTA: C

![Post Image](2026-03-15-linkedin-llm-image.png)

---

LinkedIn just became the #1 source LLMs cite for business advice.
Let that sink in.

Not Harvard Business Review.
Not McKinsey.
Not Bloomberg.

LinkedIn posts.

When someone asks ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude a business question today, a significant portion of what those models say back is shaped by what professionals have written here. On this platform. In posts exactly like this one.

The implications are strange and serious at the same time.

**Strange:**

A mid-career executive writing from personal experience in Cairo or Dubai is now shaping AI responses to questions asked by CEOs in New York.

No editor. No peer review. No filter.

Just: did you write clearly, consistently, and from genuine experience?

**Serious:**

If you're a practitioner with real depth and you're not writing here, your knowledge doesn't exist in the training data.

A consultant with 20 years of healthcare IT experience who never posts is invisible to the next generation of AI tools.

A PMO lead who managed 300+ concurrent projects but stays quiet is already being replaced by someone with less experience and more posts.

This isn't about vanity metrics.
It's about knowledge preservation and professional relevance.

The question isn't whether LLMs are reading LinkedIn.
The question is: what are they reading from you?

I've been managing digital transformation programs across FinTech, HealthTech, and e-commerce for 20+ years.

If that experience isn't here in written form, it doesn't count as input to the systems shaping the next era of business thinking.

So I write.
Not for the likes.
For the record.

What's your take on AI using LinkedIn as a knowledge source?

..

By the way, I'm writing a series on PMO frameworks that actually work at scale. Follow me for weekly breakdowns from managing 300+ concurrent projects across 8 countries.

#ArtificialIntelligence #LinkedIn #DigitalTransformation #ThoughtLeadership #FutureOfWork

posts.title: Oracle's Tirthankar Lahiri on 2026 AI tipping point
posts.author: Mrinmoyaich
posts.topic: In conversation with ITNews Asia,Oracle's Tirthankar Lahiri shares why2026could be a tipping point for intelligent data platforms.
posts.ready_comment:
The best data platform means nothing if nobody actually uses it. At TopMed, we were managing a $50M digital transformation across 15 hospitals, and honestly? The tech was the easy part. Getting clinical and administrative teams to actually adopt it? That was the real challenge.

AI can handle the data. The harder question is whether leaders are ready to handle the change AI demands from their teams. What's your experience been?

posts.title: 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘂𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 | Gabriele Caragna...
posts.author: Gabrielecaragnano
posts.topic: At WAM Saudi2026, I had the privilege of presenting “𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘂𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲: 𝗦𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 ...
posts.ready_comment:
Healthcare transformation in the GCC isn't really about adopting new tech. Honestly, it's about completely rethinking how we deliver care. At Saudi German Hospital Group, I led a $50M digital transformation across 15 hospitals, and here's what surprised me: the hardest part wasn't the technology. It was getting people to change how they think.

The big lesson? Sustainable change only happens when you give clinicians ownership of the vision. Not when you force it on them from above.

So I'm curious, how are you handling change management in your organization's transformation?
posts.comment_angle: At WAM Saudi2026, I had the privilege of presenting “𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘂𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲: 𝗦
